metadata.seed: Yeah, last week I was on my way to class and my girlfriend actually called me up saying that I forgot to take out the trash. And I kind of started thinking, wow, like, I forgot to take out the trash. Something so simple, a simple chore. All I had to do was take out the trash today and I forgot to do it. And then I started to think, well, I can't do something as simple as that. Why is she still with me? Or, and why, why can't I do that? Why can't I do a simple task? Am I good enough for anyone? Am I good enough for her? Kind of starting to feel a little fearful that she would just up and leave me.

patient.query: Yeah, last week I was on my way to class and my girlfriend actually called me up saying that I forgot to take out the trash. And I kind of started thinking, wow, like, I forgot to take out the trash. Something so simple, a simple chore. All I had to do was take out the trash today and I forgot to do it. And then I started to think, well, I can't do something as simple as that. Why is she still with me? Or, and why, why can't I do that? Why can't I do a simple task? Am I good enough for anyone? Am I good enough for her? Kind of starting to feel a little fearful that she would just up and leave me.

triggers: forgot to take out the trash
automatic_thoughts: I forgot to take out the trash
I can't do something as simple as that
Why is she still with me?
Am I good enough for anyone?
Am I good enough for her?

neutral: Forgets what was going to do (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling really anxious about forgetting the trash and what that says about you. What evidence do you have that this single mistake means she will leave you? And what evidence might suggest she still values you despite this slip?

patient.query: I don't know... I guess I just feel like every little thing adds up, you know? If I forget something like that, it makes me think I'm not good enough. But I keep coming back to moments where she reassures me, like when we laugh together or when she says she appreciates me. I just worry it'll all fall apart over something small. It's like I can't shake that feeling.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not good enough
it'll all fall apart over something small

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure over small slips, yet you also notice moments of reassurance that seem to counterbalance that worry. What evidence do you have that a single mistake will truly “fall apart” for you? How might you view those reassuring moments as evidence that you’re capable of handling small setbacks?

patient.query: I don’t know… it just feels like every little thing adds up. When I forget something, it makes me feel like I’m not good enough. Even though she reassures me sometimes, I can’t help but worry that everything will fall apart over something small. It’s like this weight I can’t shake off.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not good enough
everything will fall apart

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy sense of self‑doubt that makes even small slips feel catastrophic. What evidence do you have that forgetting something proves you’re not good enough? Could there be a more balanced way to view those moments that doesn’t let them feel like the end of everything?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel heavy sometimes. I guess when I forget things, I just remember past times when people got upset with me for being careless. It sticks with me, and I jump to thinking that if I mess up even a little, it means I'm failing. But, I don’t know… sometimes she makes me feel like I’m more than that, even if I can’t always believe it. It’s just hard to hold onto that thought when I’m feeling down.

automatic_thoughts: if I mess up even a little, it means I'm failing

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy weight whenever a small slip reminds you of past criticism. What evidence do you have that a single mistake means you’re failing? Perhaps you could view a mistake as just a moment, not a measure of your overall worth.

patient.query: I get what you're saying, but it’s tough to believe. I mean, when I think about all those times I messed up in the past, it feels like it paints this picture of who I am. It’s like I can’t separate those moments from the bigger picture. I want to, but my mind just jumps to all the proof that I’m not getting it right. It’s frustrating because I know I can do better, but then one little thing happens, and I spiral.

automatic_thoughts: I messed up in the past
this paints a picture of who I am
I can't separate those moments
I'm not getting it right
I know I can do better

neutral: Tends to fail to learn from adverse experience (finding)
Enduring personality change after catastrophic experience (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck in a cycle of past mistakes that seem to define you. You’ve mentioned you know you can do better—what evidence do you have that those moments fully capture who you are? Exploring how those experiences fit into a larger picture of growth might help you see a more balanced view.

patient.query: Yeah… I guess I just focus on the mistakes too much. It’s hard to see the good stuff when I’m in that headspace. I mean, I've had moments when I’ve been there for friends and done well at work, but they feel so far away compared to all the times I feel like I’ve let people down. It’s like I’m trying to grab hold of those positive memories, but the negatives are so loud. I know I should see it as growth, but I

automatic_thoughts: I focus on the mistakes too much
It's hard to see the good stuff
I feel like I’ve let people down
The negatives are so loud

neutral: Tends to fail to learn from adverse experience (finding)
Enduring personality change after catastrophic experience (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the negative memories feel louder than the positives. What evidence do you have that the good moments are less real or less important than the mistakes? How might you weigh the evidence for both sides when you’re in that headspace?

patient.query: It’s true, they do feel louder, like they just stick with me longer. But I guess I’m not sure how to weigh it all out. The mistakes seem clearer in my mind, like they're proof of my failures, while the good moments feel more like flukes or just nice things that happened. Maybe I forget the good ones because they don’t feel as “solid” compared to the bad ones. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle of remembering what I didn’t do

good moments feel like flukes

patient.query: I guess the mistakes feel heavy because they’re just so easy to recall. They pop into my mind like a movie playing on repeat. Maybe it’s the way I've been conditioned to think, like I should always be perfect, and anything less feels like a failure. But then there are moments when I’ve really helped a friend or done something well that made me proud. Those times, I sometimes think, “Oh, that was just luck,” which isn’t fair. It’s tough to shift my

automatic_thoughts: I should always be perfect
Anything less feels like a failure
Oh, that was just luck

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure from the belief that you must be flawless, which can make even small errors feel overwhelming. What evidence do you have that every mistake must be a sign of failure, and how might you view a slip as a chance to learn instead? Considering that moments of pride often come from genuine effort, how could you give yourself the same credit for those achievements?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel like there’s this constant pressure to be flawless, and it’s exhausting. I think I hold onto those mistakes because they seem like proof that I’m not enough, and it feels easier to believe that than to accept that I could grow from them. The idea of seeing a slip as a chance to learn is hard for me; it’s almost like my brain just defaults to punishment instead of growth. I want to give myself credit for the good moments, but it’s like

I could grow from mistakes
seeing a slip as a chance to learn is hard
my brain defaults to punishment instead of growth

llm_response.response: It sounds exhausting to feel that constant pressure to be flawless. What moments have you noticed where a mistake actually led to something positive? Maybe we can explore how your brain might shift from punishment to seeing those slips as learning opportunities.

patient.query: It really is exhausting. I guess there have been times when I’ve messed up at work and had to talk to my boss about it. But those conversations often led to some really helpful feedback that I wouldn’t have gotten otherwise. I learned from those mistakes and ended up doing better later on, but at the moment, all I could think about was how bad I felt for messing up. It’s hard to shift my focus from just feeling like a failure to recognizing those moments as steps forward. I

triggers: talking to my boss about mistakes

I messed up at work

llm_response.response: It sounds exhausting to keep feeling like a failure. What evidence do you have that you’re a failure, and what evidence suggests you’re actually learning from those mistakes? How might you view those conversations as steps forward rather than setbacks?
